exemplar.title: To what extent does meditation improve attention in adolescents?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does meditation improve attention in adolescents?

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: The topic of meditation and adolescent attention is communicated clearly and maintained throughout, with effective contextual background and purpose.
The research question is clearly stated and linked to the analysis, demonstrating a generally focused inquiry.
A range of relevant peer-reviewed studies is chosen, showing evidence of informed source selection.
The introduction and structure make the essayâs focus explicit and reader-friendly.
weaknesses: Minor lapses in precision (e.g., drifting into academic-performance claims) prevent fully sustained focus.
The declared topic title does not exactly match the content, risking reader confusion.
Search strategy, inclusion criteria and data-evaluation procedures are not explicitly described.
The broad age range and meditation types weaken the tightness of the research questionâs focus.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place the work solidly in the 5â6 band for communication of topic and purpose, and in the 3â4 band for question focus and methodology. The student excels at articulating purpose and maintaining focus, but minor imprecision and an under-developed methodology section warrant a 5 out of 6.

strengths: Demonstrates solid subject knowledge with accurate descriptions of attention networks, ERP measures and mindfulness constructs.
Consistent and mostly accurate use of specialized psychological terminology (e.g., dual n-back, dispositional mindfulness).
Integration of primary studies (Funahashi, Tang et al., Kang et al.) shows clear understanding of neuroscience and behavioral measures.
Sections on nutrition and exercise effectively apply data from multi-country studies and fitness tests.
weaknesses: Occasional reliance on secondary web sources (Psych Central, BPS blog) weakens source credibility.
Theoretical explanations can be surface-level (e.g., equating attention with consciousness too simply).
Statistical details (p-values, effect sizes) are often omitted, reducing depth of interpretation.
Informal phrasing occasionally undermines academic tone.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay fits the 3â4 band: good knowledge with mostly relevant source application and accurate terminology. Occasional imprecision in definitions, reliance on non-primary sources and lack of statistical detail prevent a top-band award, hence 4 out of 6.

strengths: Research base includes multiple reputable empirical studies, each clearly summarized in terms of sample, procedure and findings.
Critiques of study limitations (sample bias, short duration, cultural and gender issues) show emerging evaluative skill.
Connections between empirical findings and the research question are generally explicit and coherent.
Identification of confounds and suggestions for mixed-methods follow-up demonstrate analytical initiative.
weaknesses: Critical depth is uneven: some sections rest on assertion rather than detailed reasoning.
Quantitative evidence (effect sizes, reaction-time changes) is not fully incorporated to support claims.
The bidirectional ambiguity of correlational designs is acknowledged but not thoroughly addressed.
Overall evaluation in the discussion remains brief and partially repetitive of earlier critiques.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Across research relevance, depth of analysis and evaluation, the work straddles the 4â6 band. It shows clear analysis and supported conclusions but lacks sustained critical depth and comprehensive evaluation. A mid-band score of 6 out of 12 appropriately reflects these strengths and limitations.

strengths: Follows Extended Essay conventions: title page, table of contents, logical headings and numbered pages.
Word count is provided and reference list formatting is generally consistent.
Layout and structure support reader navigation with clear sectioning.
TOC accurately lists major sections and page numbers.
weaknesses: Minor inconsistencies in citation style (in-text vs. reference list) affect formatting uniformity.
Occasional line-break and indentation issues (especially in sub-sections of the TOC).
Some headings and sub-headings are not uniformly styled, affecting visual cohesion.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation meets the conventions of structure and formatting but displays minor inconsistencies that prevent a top-band award. It fits solidly in the 1â2 or 3â4 band; a score of 3 out of 4 reflects correct application with room for refinement.

strandSelections.reasoning: Key psychological terms (executive control, dispositional mindfulness, ERP, dual n-back) are used correctly and consistently.  Minor slips (e.g., equating attention simply with consciousness) and occasional informal phrasing limit full excellence, but overall terminology is mostly accurate and appropriate.

Formatting is largely correct: word count supplied, pages apparently numbered, section headings consistent, and a clearly formatted reference list.  Minor inconsistencies in citation style and occasional line-break issues prevent the very top mark but still satisfy the 3â4 band requirements.

The essay demonstrates solid psychological knowledge: accurate descriptions of attention networks, cortisol, ERP, ANT and working-memory links.  Citations are generally relevant and integrated, though occasionally secondary websites (Psych Central, BPS blog) substitute for primary literature, and theoretical explanations are sometimes surface-level.  This fits âgood knowledge with mostly relevant source applicationâ.

Methodology is essentially a literature review of six peer-reviewed studies.  Sources are largely appropriate and selected with some rationale (dates, participant ages, controls), but selection criteria, search strategy, and data-evaluation procedures are not explained.  Range is adequate but not extensive (four meditation studies, two alternatives).  These features correspond to a mostly complete but not fully developed methodology.

The student clearly states the topic (meditation and adolescent attention), provides contextual background, and outlines the essayâs purpose in the introduction and table of contents.  The focus on psychology is explicit and maintained throughout the body sections.  Minor lapses in precision (e.g., drifting into academic-performance claims not followed up later) prevent a top-of-band award, but overall communication of topic, purpose and focus is accurate and effective.

The student synthesises empirical articles from reputable journals, all directly relevant to meditation or alternative influences on attention.  Each source is summarized with sample, procedure and findings, showing clear relevance to the question.  The research base is good but not exhaustive or global enough for the top 10â12 band; hence low-7 in the 7â9 band is appropriate.

The research questionââTo what extent does meditation improve attention in adolescents?ââis clearly stated and generally kept in view.  It is, however, broad: it spans all forms of meditation and the whole 10â24 age bracket, yet the discussion centres mainly on mindfulness in late adolescence.  This partial mis-match shows an only partly focused connection, matching the 3â4 band.

The essay follows EE conventions: title page, table of contents, logical sectioning, headings and numbered pages (as implied by TOC).  All required elements appear present and in correct order, so full marks within this strand are warranted.

A reasoned overall argument is presented, culminating in the claim that meditation is effective âto an extentâ.  The student weighs alternative explanations and cross-cultural limitations, yet evaluation remains brief and largely repetitive of earlier critique.  The argument structure is clear but lacks sustained critical depth, placing it at the lower end of the 4â6 band.

The essay moves beyond description by critiquing limitations (sample bias, short duration, confounds) and strengths (objective measures, control groups).  Connections between findings and the RQ are usually explicit, but depth is uneven and sometimes rests on assertion rather than detailed reasoning.  Conclusions are mostly supported, matching the mid-band (4â6).

annotations.comment: Consider quantifying performance improvements (e.g., ERP amplitude changes or reaction times) to support claims with numerical evidence.
The Zhong et al. critique of generalizability (male-only sample) demonstrates critical awareness of cultural and gender limitations.
To deepen analysis, include statistical values (e.g., p-values, effect sizes) from ANT results rather than descriptive assertions.
Tang et al. description of IBMT versus relaxation is thorough, demonstrating accurate use of specialized terminology.
The evaluation of extraneous variables (e.g., outside events during training) is valid, but could be enriched by suggesting control measures.
Consider discussing how cultural context and educational systems might interact with meditation efficacy across studies.
The Kang et al. section correctly explains the dual n-back task and EEG outcome, reflecting clear terminology use.
Critique of practice effects and lack of qualitative data highlights understanding of potential confounds; suggest proposing mixed-methods follow-up.
The physical exercise section conveys a clear correlation between fitness tests and attention, integrating Reigal et al.âs findings effectively.
The student correctly provides the report title at the start, meeting the formatting requirement and clearly orienting the reader.
The declared topic (âFactors Affecting Academic Performanceâ) does not align with the actual focus on meditation and attention, risking reader confusion. Refine to match content.
Including a word count at the start meets IB formatting requirements and helps verify adherence to the 4000-word limit.
The table of contents is clearly organised with all major sections and page numbers, supporting reader navigation and demonstrating appropriate structure.
Ensure sub-section indentations under âAlternative Explanationsâ are consistent to improve clarity in the table of contents.
Opening paragraph equates attention with consciousness too simplistically. Incorporate nuanced definitions and cite primary literature for a more precise conceptual grounding.
The explanation of Knudsenâs four components of attention is accurate and well integrated, demonstrating clear subject understanding.
The introduction describes mindfulness but does not link directly to the research questionâs scope. Tighten the narrative to foreshadow later analysis.
The research question is clearly stated and linked to later sections, but its broad age range and meditation types weaken focus. Consider narrowing age or technique.
Switch to third-person phrasing throughout to maintain formal academic tone and consistency.
The neuroscience section correctly identifies the prefrontal cortexâs role in attention, reflecting solid integration of Funahashiâs work.
Discussion of volumetric changes after meditation (Fox et al.) is well placed but would benefit from specifying effect magnitudes.
There is no explicit methodology section; explain search strategies, inclusion criteria, and data-evaluation procedures to strengthen method transparency.
Summaries of empirical studies (e.g., Sanger et al.) include design, sample and findings, demonstrating good research relevance.
The critique of short-term intervention and lack of follow-up shows emerging evaluation skills, but depth remains uneven.
Use of objective ERP measures and a control group is a clear strength, raising the internal validity of the Sanger et al. study discussion.
The correlational design is acknowledged, but bidirectional ambiguity remains unaddressed. Recommend suggesting an experimental follow-up study.
The nutrition section employs robust multi-country data and correctly links dietary patterns to attention capacity.
Conclusion restates main studies but omits explicit reflection on research question scope mismatch; tighten to directly address broad age and technique limitations.
References display mixed citation formats and missing access dates; apply a consistent style guide (e.g., APA) and complete all entries.
Reliance on secondary web sources for theoretical definitions (e.g., Psych Central) weakens source credibility; prioritize peer-reviewed literature.
